question_title: Will Google Chrome Stable release notes mention a vulnerability exploited in the wild before May 1, 2026?
summary: Chrome 146 Stable channel update (March 12, 2026) patched CVE-2026-3909 and CVE-2026-3910, both confirmed by Google as actively exploited in the wild. Multiple independent security outlets quote the Chrome Releases blog post. This falls within the Feb 24 - May 1 resolution window. The event has almost certainly already occurred. Minor uncertainty remains because the fetch tool didn't fully render the blog post's security section, but third-party corroboration is overwhelming. Even if it somehow didn't qualify, the ~7 weeks remaining would provide additional opportunities given Chrome's high base rate of ~8 in-the-wild zero-days per year.

factors.description: Chrome 146 Stable update (March 12, 2026) patched CVE-2026-3909 and CVE-2026-3910 confirmed exploited in the wild by multiple credible security sources quoting Google
March 12, 2026 is within the resolution window (Feb 24 - May 1, 2026)
Direct fetch of Chrome Releases blog did not fully render the 'exploited in the wild' text due to content truncation - primary source not directly verified
Even without the March 12 event, Chrome's high base rate (~8 zero-days/year in 2025) gives strong probability of at least one in-the-wild exploit disclosure in the remaining ~7 weeks to May 1

reasoning:
**Forecast: 0.97**

The Chrome 146 Stable channel update posted March 12, 2026 patched two zero-days (CVE-2026-3909 and CVE-2026-3910) that Google confirmed are actively exploited in the wild. This falls squarely within the Feb 24 – May 1 resolution window. Multiple independent security outlets (CyberInsider, GBHackers, SOC Prime, CyberPress) all quote Google's Chrome Releases blog post using "exploited in the wild" language.

I set the probability at 0.97 rather than 0.99 because my direct fetches of the Chrome Releases blog didn't fully render the security details (likely a content truncation issue), so I couldn't personally verify the exact wording on the primary resolution source. However, the third-party corroboration is overwhelming, and even without this event, Chrome's historical zero-day frequency (~8/year in 2025) makes at least one qualifying disclosure highly likely in the remaining 7 weeks.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will Google Chrome Stable release notes mention a vulnerability exploited in the wild before May 1, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/42512)
[Google patches two actively exploited zero-days in Chrome 146 update](https://cyberinsider.com/google-patches-two-actively-exploited-zero-days-in-chrome-146-update/)
resolution_reason: The question resolves YES. Multiple credible sources confirm that on March 12-13, 2026, Google released a Stable Channel Update for Desktop that explicitly stated: "Google is aware that exploits for both CVE-2026-3909 & CVE-2026-3910 exist in the wild." This post is dated within the required window (February 24, 2026 to May 1, 2026) and includes the exact language about vulnerabilities being exploited in the wild. The BleepingComputer article quotes Google's security advisory directly: "Google is aware that exploits for both CVE-2026-3909 & CVE-2026-3910 exist in the wild." This language clearly indicates vulnerabilities were exploited in the wild, satisfying the resolution criteria.
resolution_criteria: This question resolves to yes if any Chrome Releases blog post for the Stable channel dated on or after February 24, 2026 and before May 1, 2026 includes language indicating a vulnerability was exploited in the wild (or equivalent wording). This question resolves to no otherwise. The primary source is the Chrome Releases blog at https://chromereleases.googleblog.com/
fine_print: Count only Stable channel release posts (exclude Beta/Dev if clearly labeled). Use the post date on the blog. If wording disputes arise, resolve using credible sources per https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions Candidate Sources: Chrome Releases “Stable updates” label view (backup navigation) https://chromereleases.googleblog.com/search/label/Stable%20updates | Chrome desktop release notes on chromium.org (backup) https://chromium.org
background_info:
This forecasts whether Chrome will patch an in-the-wild exploited bug soon, relevant for browser patch urgency and exploit trends.
